summary: Severe memory latency bottleneck due to inefficient global memory access patterns causing long scoreboard stalls
reasoning: The kernel shows 92.85% compute_memory_throughput but only 1.45% DRAM throughput, indicating memory operations are bottlenecked at L1 cache level. The 55.47% long scoreboard stalls confirm warps are waiting for memory operations. Despite 98.37% L1 hit rate, the 7.4% global load efficiency and 93.85% L1 throughput suggest poor memory coalescing is causing excessive cache line fetches.

root_causes.cause: Extremely poor global memory load efficiency causing excessive memory transactions

evidence.interpretation: Only 7.4% of loaded bytes are actually used, meaning ~13x more data is fetched than needed due to uncoalesced access patterns
L1 cache is nearly saturated processing redundant memory requests from poor coalescing

root_causes.cause: High memory latency stalls due to long dependency chains in scattered memory accesses

evidence.interpretation: Over half of warp cycles are stalled waiting for memory operations to complete
Very low warp occupancy (12.48%) limits ability to hide memory latency through parallelism

recommended_fixes.fix: Restructure memory access to load contiguous tiles into shared memory before computation. Preload x_tile and w_tile for multiple kernel positions using cooperative loading across threads, then perform computation from shared memory to improve coalescing from 7.4% to near 100%.
recommended_fixes.rationale: Current scattered access pattern (id_val, ih_val, iw_val computed per-thread) causes uncoalesced loads. Using shared memory staging with coalesced global loads will dramatically reduce L1 pressure and memory transactions, directly addressing both the poor load efficiency and long scoreboard stalls.
